speaker: interviewer
text: It's on the 25th of February, 1997, by Nina Elazar-Wolff in Bat Yam, Israel. The language is English. In the last cassette, we interrupted. And we were talking about Vaboulis' work, where he could got-- get some [NON-ENGLISH] for money.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. So he-- those old Russian people from the west, from the eastern part of Latvia, near the Russian border, used to come to our house very, very early in the morning. And they brought us-- they brought the carton, and they paid him. And we used to save the money up for case where we could escape, because he was looking all the time for a possibility to escape Latvia. But that was almost impossible. Until, one nice day, he found a person who knew Latvian fishermen. They owned a boat which was old and not too safe. And they promised, for big money and for a lot of drinks, to bring us over to Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to travel-- it was not from Riga-- to the harbor of Windau City. And there, they got drunk, and they said to me, the men, all right. But we are not going to take a girl with us. And the men were all people who were deserters of the German army. Innocent people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason Vaboulis wanted to escape Latvia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, he didn't want to fight. People were mobilized. He didn't want to fight against the-- against the Russians in the German army. And the second reason was I were there. He didn't have a shelter for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, don't-- don't you worry. I promise you I will not cry. And it took us several days until they got absolutely drunk and forgot who they are and where they are. And we didn't know whether they are going to take us to Windau, because we had already paid-- towards Sweden. Because we had already paid them a big sum of money. And finally, they decided that the evening has come. We have to-- to go to Sweden. So we were crawling through the sand, and there was a German a lighthouse with German soldiers. A whole story. So to make a long story short, we escaped that, too. And we were taken in a very small boat-- I believe we were 14 people in a very small boat-- small boat, some kilometers into the sea, where their boat was waiting for us. Which date was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 23rd of April, 1944. And the sea was rough in April. And we were 16 hours on our way to Sweden. It was a very dramatic escape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were moments when we thought they were so drunk that they are taking us back to Latvia. Then there was a moment when overhead we noticed a German airplane. And through the cockpit we could see the contours of the head of the pilot. And all of us, including me, were absolutely sure that we are going to be shot. Again, there was a case where I can't explain rationally how it happened that we stayed alive. Perhaps the German pilot took us for Swedish for Swedish fishermen who had lost their way. Perhaps they took us for refugees who were fighting for their life. One of our friends was holding my slip, which was light blue, in his hand, to show them that it-- that it's a Swedish flag. Perhaps it had no meaning at all. I can't tell you. But the fact is that the airplane disappeared, thanks to my [NON-ENGLISH], probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened? Who were the people with you on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Those who had been in the-- mobilized forcefully in the German army and ran away, escaped. Young men. Two were students of theology, innocent people, absolutely. A little crazy. There was [? Nanc's ?] friend, the man I mentioned, [INAUDIBLE], who was also an officer, a very handsome man. Absolutely innocent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, there were no Jew killers and no-- no fighters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we arrived in Sweden. On the Swedish coast, I got-- I caught sight of a German officer in-- in a German uniform. I was terribly scared. And then I noticed that he had his wedding-- that he was wearing his wedding ring on his left finger. On the finger on the left hand, and the Germans did not. And since my father had lived in Sweden, he told me that the Swedes wear rings on their left hand. So I understood it was a-- it was a Swedish officer whose uniform was very similar to the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they waved, as they understood we were the first the refugees. And they saw a young woman, so they were very friendly with us. We were checked by-- by the doctor. We were given a bath. And that was in Visby, the isle of Visby. And then we went-- were taken further to Stockholm. And all of us were placed in a [NON-ENGLISH], a quarantine. And since I spoke English and German, I had an easier time. I explained to them whom I was and the whole truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: They let me go to Stockholm in the first place. And what can a Jewish girl do in Stockholm in the first place? I looked for a Jewish-- for a synagogue to see a Jewish living person. That was the day of my life, when I came to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got acquainted with a Latvian Jew who had gone there in the late '30s with his family. And he was a representative for a World Jewish Congress in-- in Sweden. And very friendly, and et cetera, et cetera. And now he said, now you have to leave the boy and marry a Jewish man. And I said, probably yes, but we are not going to hurry with that. I want to find a job. [CHUCKLES] And he took me to the American Embassy, where I met [NON-ENGLISH], who was very sincere and friendly from the very first moment he saw me. And he understood that I needed work. So he gave me translations for the embassy, German-English, English-German. I believe about [INAUDIBLE], if I'm not mistaken, too. And there we got a very nice small apartment we hired. And we were living there. I was going to university. The first week I came to Sweden, I wanted to go on to continue my studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came to the university, and Professor Gabrielson said, where are you from? I said, I'm from Riga, from the English college. Do you know Professor [? Muller? ?] I said, that is my teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you don't need any examinations. If he's your professor, you don't need any examinations. So I didn't have to pass any examinations. And I studied German and-- Professor [INAUDIBLE]-- studied German and-- and English. And besides, I worked also in the university library. Very interesting job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived to the Swedish coast, what time of the day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have seen the-- the officer if it were night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember the first feeling of the first time your foot--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was on Swedish soil?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. Finally, I am free. But on the-- on the boat, I did not have the feeling. I thought they are taking us back. We have paid them the money, and they're taking us back. Finally, I am free. And so I didn't have to live in any camps, like the others. I lived a-- we lived in a [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH], it's called, in Stockholm-- a suburb. A beautiful suburb.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the quarantine?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day or two. No more. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the point of your social point of view, how did you go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, I found as many Jewish people as I could. I got to [? a ?] [NON-ENGLISH], and-- well, and there were, you know, I was young then, and-- and I was having a good time. Vaboulis was staying at home. He didn't go anywhere. He was depressed. I was going with my-- out with my Jewish friends, making trips and going to different parties and working hard. Working hard. And at home, we invited people. Only his friends. His friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Latvians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, very, very many boats arrived, one after the other. And I saw in the streets Latvians wearing German uniforms, [NON-ENGLISH]. So they arrived in-- Jew killers-- they arrived in the German uniforms, of the SS [NON-ENGLISH]. And after I had heard from one of our guests that they had a drowned a Jew whom they had picked up in Windau in order to take him to Sweden, and then they had second thoughts, and they decided to show him the Swedish the Swedish land in the distance and, look, Jew. That is-- that is Sweden. That is freedom. And they threw him into the sea. I have forgotten his name. I knew his name. I rushed to the Swedish police, and I informed about that fact. And I mentioned the name of the people who told me, the man, exactly, who told me. And then I started looking for-- I was traveling from camp to camp, looking for these people, searching for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Latvian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only because they are Latvians, Because they were in uniform, because I knew who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you marry Vaboulis then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not there. I didn't marry. I did not have the intention to marry him at all, because I had my family in-- in Scotland. I kept in touch with them. Immediately, I took up touch with them. And they wanted me to come after the war, because it was impossible to travel during the war. So-- but then the letter from Palestine arrived, which decided my further fate. So now in a few words, perhaps, about the reason why I came-- why I went back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, perhaps you've got something else to ask me. Please.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can describe your work and life in Sweden--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. So, in the first place, it was university. At university, I got a scholarship of 200 crowns monthly. Part-- part-- I had a part-time job at the university library. Mr. [? Muleen ?] was my boss. And he used to-- to carry me from-- to carry me-- to take me from one room to the other to show me as if I was an ape, a monkey. Here is a young lady of 23 who knows so and so many languages. They don't know languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I felt very embarrassed about that. But I had to sort out different books because it's-- there is a habit in Sweden that if rich people die, they contribute all they have at home, particularly the books, to the Swedish university. And this is what-- this was my job. There were even Yiddish books and Hebrew books, et cetera, et cetera. But I found it very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked for the American embassy, but I did the translations at home. So I made a living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've always had some friends from before the war who lived-- who worked at the Latvian-- free Latvian embassy from before the war. He had there, in Stockholm, so we were on friendly terms with them. They were very nice and very decent, honest people. The lady herself was Swiss. She gave me French lessons. I gave her English lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to meet people. And we threw parties at home, as well, because they were-- Sweden-- Stockholm was a very interesting city during the war. You know, all the espionage and everything went to the neutral country. And there were refugees from all over the world, from all over Europe, I would say. So it was an interesting time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was engaged, again, with a Jewish problem. So, and here, I would have to mention the letter. As soon as I escaped to Sweden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an article in New York, in South Africa, and in Palestine was published that a young Jewish girl named Zelda Shebshelovitz has escaped to Sweden in a fishing boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were already many Latvian youngsters who had come to Israel before the war. Is [INAUDIBLE] among them? [? Pfft. ?] Half of my [INAUDIBLE]. Well, I got so many letters, you can't even-- you wouldn't believe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the letters took me by surprise. It was written by Frieda Hirschowitz. The girl's name was Hirschowitz. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it's H-I-R-S-C-H-O-W-I-T-Z. Hirschowitz. Frieda Hirschowitz, from a very Zionist family, a wealthy family. She was my brother's fiance. And so she decided to come earlier to Israel because she had the money for the certificate. That was, I believe, in '38 or '39 she came. And my brother-- or even earlier. My Brother was supposed to follow her as soon as he had the money. And in the meantime, the war broke out. So they were separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, she took me for dead. And when she learned that I'm alive, she wrote me the letter, because in the newspaper, my address was given. She wrote me a letter telling me that my brother was alive. He's not dead. He's in Siberia, et cetera, et cetera. And this was my decision, already, to, as soon as I can, to go-- as soon as the war is over, to go home and to see what's-- what's happening to him, the only survivor of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it, time-wise, when you got the letter, that you knew the brother is alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. I think it was summer, 1944. Several months after we arrived. I had already the feeling that I have to return.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know, at that stage, everything? What happened to the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Community where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Latvia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SCOFFS] I knew about it already in Riga, because I was there. So in Sweden, I wrote a report in the German language, [NON-ENGLISH], of 55 pages, from the 1st of July, et cetera, et cetera. Friends translated it into English. I don't know why I didn't do it myself. I took the translation to Mr. Olsen, to the American Embassy, one to the English, British Embassy, and to the World Jewish Congress, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some Latvian former diplomats went on working as-- had responsible jobs in different Swedish establishments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They read the report. They were terribly frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a list of a number of war criminals. They destroyed it. There was a list of survivors, which they destroyed partly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, after the war, I thought I would never see it again. And on one occasion, when a certain Sir Hedgrinden from Britain came to Israel for this purpose, he brought-- he brought my report, which I eventually saw on the writing desk. And he asked me, do you know what I'm reading? I say, yes. I do. What's that? I say, that is my report I had written and taken to your embassy. He says, how do you know? I say, I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he-- see, he asked me whether I would be ready to go to Britain, since there are a number of Latvian war criminals living in Britain and free, if I would be ready to come and testify if I knew the people. I said, if need be. He was the British secretary. He was a very nice, friendly man. In the evening, he returned to London. And I asked him in a letter the following day that-- I had-- I realized that I had done a stupid thing-- thing. I should have asked him immediately to send me a copy, which I didn't do, because I was very upset. And he sent me the copy immediately, for which I am very thankful to him. Now it is in my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wrote the report in '44. And how many years later did you get a copy of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a copy several years ago. Half a century or so. It took half a century. But this is-- the most important thing in this story is that nobody in Stockholm, neither is American nor in the British embassy, believed. The Jewish children and women are being killed, and I cried it out to all the world. There's a-- This I have to tell, mention, that the most important newspaper in Sweden is It's a right-wing paper, very popular, like [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]. I had a report for them, too, a different one, a short one. I went there to the editor, to the chief editor. And that was a time when they still hoped that the Germans would win the war. And when I told him the contents of my report, he said, I am sorry. For such things, we have no space in our newspaper. That gives you a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was furious, in the first place. I was able to kill him for that. That he has no place, no space, as he said. I remember his face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till when did you stay in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe May '45. As soon as the war was over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took the decision to return.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take the decision together, you and Vaboulis?

speaker: interviewee
text: To do what?

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- did you take the decision to go back to home, to Riga--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --together with Vaboulis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I would never have gone without him. I wouldn't. I wouldn't have left him.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that stage, what kind of relationship you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends. He was-- to me, he always was a man who has saved my life and risked his own, including this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he still in love with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. He's 83, but I think he's still in love with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children went to see him, I told you, recently. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you leave Sweden in May '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you go to where? Where do you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, we were kept in-- in-- by the Russians in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, again, it was Visby. No. What was the name of the city which-- Viberg. Viberg, which belonged to Finland some time ago-- they had occupied it. It was a terrible camp. And there were some Jewish officers who knew that I was a Jewish girl. And they told me [NON-ENGLISH]. They knew. They were Chekists. They knew that something terrible is going to happen to me because, those days, if someone came from abroad or knew languages, he's a Finnish person. So they were very nice to me, but they couldn't help me. We went back to Riga. And immediately, the KGB started hunting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at that stage already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- I got married on the 27th of August, 1945. And only for one simple reason. Because Latvian people-- all non-Jewish people, actually, believed that if one marries, in the Soviet Union, a Jewish, or a Jew, his life is saved, that they're very-- the Jewish people have a lot to say in the country, and it's-- which is not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let us come back. You arrive in Riga in August, in July?

speaker: interviewee
text: May.

speaker: interviewer
text: May '45. You start living your normal life to start to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm staying. I'm going to Goldingen to see what's happening there in my shtetel, and I found [? that ?] [? going ?] [? on ?] living in my house, and all our furniture, all our things. And they didn't-- they didn't want to-- even to hear anything about the apartment. And we went to the Jewish cemetery again and wanted to see whether the graves still exist. And then we saw there is no staying in Goldingen anymore, that we have to go back to Riga. We-- we understood that we are lost people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any family, further family of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody was dead. I went back to college-- which was not a college anymore, which was an institute of the English language-- in order to pass my final examinations, which I hadn't passed. So, while--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to Ieva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, well, she was all right. She was in the countryside. And when I came to Riga, she came to stay with us. Yes. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were wanted. We were kept in the KGB for days and nights and asked questions about people whom we had never seen and never heard of. They never incriminated us anything we have done during the time of the German occupation, because they knew exactly that this is [? kosher. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend went to testify for [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, yeah. Vaboulis is a man who has helped so many Jews. The Chekists said, we are not going to punish him for-- for helping Jews. We are going to punish him for what he has done during the Soviet occupation against us, against the Cheka.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was arrested on the 10th-- [INAUDIBLE] on the 1st of February, 1946. Not arrested, but detained. He was arrested the first one. And then they followed me. They wanted to see who I meet, what people I associate with. And then they took me for 10 days' detention in the cellars of the KGB, which was a horrible time. And on the 10th of February, which is the Red Army Day, they let me out. And I was so weakened and so frightened that I didn't know where to go home. I didn't know the way to go home. Absolutely exhausted, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they followed me a long time, wanted to see what I'm doing. You know what [? they're ?] [NON-ENGLISH]. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so what happened next? But how did you meet your-- did you meet your brother in the meantime, since you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother came to Riga to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before you were arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. And we went to the prosecutor, to the public prosecutor, [INAUDIBLE], because [? Nanc ?] was already in prison then. And in all seriousness, I said to the [INAUDIBLE], he is an innocent man, but he has saved my life. And I, as a Jewess, am coming to you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take me instead of him, because I am as innocent as he is. But I'm ready to go to prison for him. And please release him. He-- [? Bohomof ?] was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: It has fallen down. Something has fallen down here. No? No. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? Is that all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Nothing fell down. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have trod on something.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Go on. It's OK. Don't worry. Go on, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think-- yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. So he answered me in Russian, [NON-ENGLISH]-- if you really ask me, we will arrest you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, he saw a stupid, naive person. I didn't know what the Soviet system is like. I didn't know anything what they're able to do. That was something new to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went home. And it took a long time. A long time. I don't remember how many months. In the meantime, I-- on the first of May, my friends came to my house-- friends. And they brought Grishe, my present husband, with them. And Vaboulis was still in jail at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He still was and remained, and there was no hope he would get out of jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he accused for?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was accused of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] being a traitor of his homeland. And later, it was changed-- the code was changed to anti-Soviet propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were husband and wife at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were already husband and wife. Yes. Because we got married in August of '45, and that was already [? forty ?] summer-- it was the first of May, 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my friends came to me. We had just had a cup of coffee or some-- a glass of wine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sometimes went out with Grishe, but we were just on friendly terms. Not on too friendly terms, just acquaintances. [INAUDIBLE] I could speak German and speak Yiddish and about, you know, Jewish questions, et cetera. And then they were-- then they started persecuting-- persecuting me in a terrible way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Calling me, summoning me to the KGB very often, even over the nights, and questioning me, investigating about things I had never seen and heard of. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the meantime, Vaboulis got 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sentenced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in the tribunal. You couldn't-- there was no access. So I took off my gold bracelet, gave to a Russian soldier, and he gave me the opportunity to see him for two or three minutes. So I found out that he got 10 years. Now, [INAUDIBLE] is a terrible camp, one of the most terrible camps in the Soviet Union. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come to-- to the end of this cassette, and we'll go on in the next one in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
